title: Unemployment, Inflation, AD/AS and Policy
summary: Unemployment types, inflation, the AD/AS model, monetary policy (cash rate), fiscal policy (government spending and taxes), and the multiplier effect.
content_html:
Unemployment, Inflation, AD/AS and Policy

These are the four major macroeconomic issues: economic growth, unemployment, inflation, and the balance of payments.

Unemployment

Unemployment rate: % of the labour force that is unemployed (actively seeking work and available to start immediately).

Labour force: Employed + Unemployed. Excludes discouraged workers (gave up looking).

Types of unemployment:

  • Frictional: Between jobs for normal reasons (searching for better match).
  • Structural: Caused by shifts in demand or technology making some skills obsolete.
  • Natural unemployment: Frictional + Structural. Essentially steady over the business cycle. NAIRU: Non-accelerating inflation rate of unemployment.
  • Cyclical: Varies with the business cycle -- rises in recession, falls in expansion.
  • Actual unemployment = Natural + Cyclical.

The AD/AS Model

The AD/AS model explains short-run fluctuations in real GDP and the price level.

  • Aggregate Demand (AD): Total planned expenditure (C + I + G + NX) at various price levels. Downward-sloping due to wealth effect, interest-rate effect, and international-trade effect.
  • Short-Run Aggregate Supply (SRAS): Upward-sloping -- firms produce more in response to higher prices in the short run.
  • Equilibrium: AD intersects SRAS to determine real GDP and the price level.

Monetary Policy

Set by the Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA). Objective: stability of currency, full employment, economic prosperity, inflation between 2-3% on average over the business cycle.

  • Expansionary monetary policy: RBA decreases the cash rate. Other interest rates fall. Investment, consumption, and net exports rise. AD shifts right. GDP and price level rise.
  • Contractionary monetary policy: RBA increases the cash rate. Other interest rates rise. Investment, consumption, and net exports fall. AD shifts left. GDP and price level fall.

Fiscal Policy

Changes in federal taxes and purchases intended to achieve macroeconomic objectives.

  • Expansionary fiscal policy: Increase government spending OR cut taxes. AD shifts right. Used to combat recession.
  • Contractionary fiscal policy: Cut government spending OR increase taxes. AD shifts left. Used to combat inflation.
  • Automatic stabilisers: Transfer payments and taxes that automatically adjust with the business cycle (e.g. unemployment benefits rise in recession without new legislation).

The Multiplier Effect

An increase in autonomous expenditure (e.g. government spending) increases GDP by MORE than the initial amount, because spending creates income which creates more spending.

Multiplier = 1 / (1 - MPC), where MPC = Marginal Propensity to Consume.

exercises.title: Unemployment rate definition
exercises.prompt: The unemployment rate measures the percentage of:
options: The total population that is not working
The labour force that is unemployed (seeking work and available to start)
All people aged 15+ who do not have a job
The workforce employed part-time
exercises.answer: The labour force that is unemployed (seeking work and available to start)

exercises.title: Structural unemployment
options: People between jobs for normal reasons
The economy being in a recession
Shifts in demand or technology making some skills obsolete
Seasonal fluctuations in employment
exercises.answer: Shifts in demand or technology making some skills obsolete

exercises.title: Expansionary monetary policy
exercises.prompt: Expansionary monetary policy involves the RBA:
options: Increasing the cash rate to reduce inflation
Decreasing the cash rate to stimulate AD
Increasing government spending
Reducing income taxes
exercises.answer: Decreasing the cash rate to stimulate AD

exercises.title: Contractionary fiscal policy
exercises.prompt: Contractionary fiscal policy is used to combat:
options: Recession and unemployment
Rising inflation when the economy is above full employment
Deflation
A current account deficit
exercises.answer: Rising inflation when the economy is above full employment

exercises.title: AD downward slope reason
exercises.prompt: Why does the aggregate demand curve slope downward?
options: Because firms produce less at higher prices
Because higher prices reduce wealth, raise interest rates, and make exports less competitive
Because government spending falls as prices rise
Because supply always falls when prices rise
exercises.answer: Because higher prices reduce wealth, raise interest rates, and make exports less competitive

exercises.title: Automatic stabilisers
exercises.prompt: Automatic stabilisers are fiscal policy tools that:
options: Require a new law each time they are activated
Automatically increase or decrease along with the business cycle without new legislation
Only affect the supply side of the economy
Are controlled by the Reserve Bank
exercises.answer: Automatically increase or decrease along with the business cycle without new legislation

exercises.title: Multiplier effect
exercises.prompt: The multiplier effect in fiscal policy means that an increase in government spending:
options: Increases GDP by exactly the same amount as the spending
Has no effect on GDP
Increases GDP by MORE than the initial spending due to induced expenditure
Only affects the price level, not GDP
exercises.answer: Increases GDP by MORE than the initial spending due to induced expenditure

exercises.title: NAIRU definition
exercises.prompt: NAIRU (Non-accelerating inflation rate of unemployment) refers to:
options: The maximum unemployment rate in a recession
The level of unemployment below which inflation would be expected to rise
The rate of unemployment when the economy is in depression
The cyclical unemployment rate
exercises.answer: The level of unemployment below which inflation would be expected to rise
